zh_name: 传输流的全变差稳定性恒等式

source_blocks: Lemma 5.1 gives a differential identity for d/dt TV(X_t,Y_t) in terms of div(tilde v - v) and <grad log p, tilde v - v>.
The proof writes both laws through the Riemannian continuity equation, differentiates the positive part of p - q, and integrates by parts.

source_span: Lemma 5.1 and proof: TV derivative under two deterministic manifold transports.
why_reusable: This is a portable stability lemma for comparing two ODE-driven probability flows in a strong distributional metric.
input: Two time-dependent vector fields on a manifold
Laws p_t and q_t induced by the two ODEs
Continuity equation for both transported densities
Regularity sufficient for integration by parts
output: Differential identity or inequality for d TV(p_t,q_t) / dt
Two proof obligations: divergence mismatch and score-weighted vector mismatch
assumptions: The flows admit densities with enough smoothness
Boundary terms vanish or the test functions have compact support
The reference density p_t has a square-integrable score where used
Vector-field mismatch has integrable divergence
core_steps: Write the two density evolutions using the intrinsic continuity equation.
Differentiate the positive part integral defining TV.
Subtract the two continuity equations and isolate the mismatch vector field.
Integrate by parts on the region where p_t - q_t is positive.
Rewrite grad p as p grad log p.
Apply absolute values and Cauchy-Schwarz to obtain a usable inequality.
limitations: Requires density regularity and careful handling of boundaries or noncompact support.
The inequality is only useful after separate score and divergence estimates.
Manifold versions require intrinsic divergence, gradient, and volume form conventions.

zh_name: 流形欧拉格式的平行移动插值

source_blocks: Section 5.1 explains that a naive Euler interpolation freezes a velocity in T_{y_k}M, but an ODE needs a velocity in T_{y_t}M.
The remedy defines tilde v(t,y_t) by parallel-transporting the frozen velocity along the interpolation geodesic, then uses the inverse of F_{t,h} to express tilde v as a function of (t,x).

source_span: Continuous-time interpolation of manifold Euler steps using Exp, inverse footpoint recovery, and parallel transport.
why_reusable: This pattern converts a discrete manifold integrator into a continuous ODE so flow-stability lemmas can be applied.
input: Manifold Euler update x_{k+1} = Exp_{x_k}(h b(t_k,x_k))
Vector field values attached to changing tangent spaces
A step-size condition making the interpolation map invertible
output: An interpolated vector field tilde v(t,x) defined intrinsically on the manifold
A bridge from discrete Euler analysis to continuous transport stability
assumptions: The exponential-map step stays within an injectivity/invertibility regime
Parallel transport is defined along the interpolation geodesic
The vector field is bounded and has controlled covariant derivative
The manifold geometry admits the needed comparison estimates
core_steps: Represent each Euler segment as a geodesic from the previous grid point.
Parallel-transport the frozen velocity along that geodesic to the current tangent space.
Define F_{t,h}(x) = Exp_x(h b(t,x)).
Prove or assume F_{t,h} is invertible for the step-size range.
Recover the previous grid point by F^{-1} and define tilde v(t,x) using that preimage.
Apply continuous-time ODE stability tools to the interpolated process.
limitations: High risk if F_{t,h} is not injective.
Requires explicit parallel transport or a way to bound its derivatives.
Cut locus and conjugate points can invalidate the construction.

zh_name: Jacobi 场步长可逆性控制

source_blocks: Lemma 5.2 gives step-size requirements that guarantee F_{t,h}(x) = Exp_x(h b(x)) is invertible.
The proof computes dF_h through a geodesic variation, identifies the variation field as a Jacobi field, parallel-transports it back, and lower-bounds ||dF_h(x)v|| using curvature comparison.

source_span: Lemma 5.2 and proof: use Jacobi fields and curvature comparison to keep one-step manifold maps invertible.
why_reusable: Many manifold discretization proofs require showing a numerical one-step map does not fold; this gives an operational proof skeleton.
input: Map F_h(x) = Exp_x(h b(x))
Bound on ||b|| and covariant derivative of b
Injectivity radius and curvature bounds
Curvature operator bound
output: Step-size restriction ensuring dF_h is nonsingular
Invertibility of the local interpolation map
Curvature-dependent constants for positive, zero, or negative lower curvature
assumptions: The step remains inside the injectivity radius
The vector field b is bounded and covariantly Lipschitz
Jacobi comparison applies up to the relevant time
Curvature bounds are known
core_steps: Differentiate F_h by introducing a variation through geodesics.
Show the differential is the endpoint of a Jacobi field.
Parallel-transport the Jacobi field to a fixed tangent space.
Use the Jacobi equation and curvature bounds to control the deviation from v + h nabla_v b.
Lower-bound the endpoint norm by triangle inequality.
Choose h so the perturbation cannot cancel the original tangent vector.
limitations: Technically delicate and geometry-dependent.
The sufficient step-size bound may be conservative.
Requires checking cut-locus and curvature assumptions.

source_blocks: The proof of Theorem 1 integrates Lemma 5.1, bounds the score-vector mismatch and divergence mismatch, and uses sum_k int_{t_k}^{t_{k+1}} (t - t_k) dt = h^2/2 to isolate h and epsilon terms.
The resulting bound is TV(pi_T, pi_hat_T) <= h C_Lip + epsilon C_eps, with constants assembled from score, vector-field, divergence, and curvature regularity.

source_span: Main theorem proof: integrate TV stability and aggregate local h and epsilon contributions into global rate constants.
why_reusable: This is a compact recipe for turning local consistency, score, and divergence estimates into a global total-variation rate.
input: Differential TV stability inequality
Bounds for score-weighted vector mismatch
Bounds for divergence mismatch
Step-size schedule and early stopping time
output: Separated numerical h and learning epsilon contributions
assumptions: Initial laws are coupled or equal
Local bounds hold uniformly or in expectation over the time interval
The time-step integral terms are summable
Early stopping avoids terminal singularity
core_steps: Start from the integrated TV stability inequality.
Insert vector-mismatch and divergence-mismatch bounds.
Compute the grid integral of local truncation terms.
Use Nh <= T to simplify accumulated constants.
Group h-dependent and epsilon-dependent terms.
Name aggregate constants for readability and theorem statement.
limitations: The quality of the result depends entirely on the local estimates.
May hide large constants in aggregate notation.
Nonconstant step schedules require recomputing the summation.

zh_name: 流形 score 正则性归约

source_blocks: Section 5.2.1 identifies score regularity E||grad log p_t(X_t)||^2 <= L_t^score as a key input.
Proposition 5.3 handles compact manifolds, while Proposition 5.4 handles Hadamard manifolds using conditional density decompositions and moment controls.

source_span: Score regularity reductions: isolate square-integrability of grad log p_t as a reusable proof obligation, then verify it by geometry-specific estimates.
why_reusable: Many distributional stability proofs require score regularity; this pattern separates the abstract need from geometry-specific verification.
input: Interpolating density p_t on a manifold
Formula or bounds for conditional densities
Geometry-specific smoothness or moment assumptions
Desired time-dependent score bound
output: Conditions under which the TV stability proof is usable
A checklist for compact versus noncompact geometry
assumptions: p_t is positive and smooth enough for grad log p_t
Cut-locus issues are controlled or absent
Noncompact cases have sufficient moment/exponential moment control
Derivative bounds for Exp/Log/Jacobian terms are available
core_steps: Identify score square-integrability as a separate lemma.
For compact manifolds, use bounded geometry and density smoothness.
For Hadamard manifolds, use absence of cut points and moment-weighted bounds.
Decompose grad log p_t into conditional density or Jacobian terms.
Bound each term using comparison geometry and moment assumptions.
Feed L_t^score into the global TV estimate.
limitations: Broad extraction spanning many technical lemmas; should be checked by a domain expert before reuse.
Concrete bounds are geometry-specific.
Failure of positivity or smoothness of p_t breaks the skill.

zh_name: SPD 流形曲率矩正则化

source_blocks: Proposition B.6 imposes exponential moment conditions and a Riemannian Gaussian prior on SPD(n), then lists expected regularity bounds for v, grad v, time derivatives, and grad div v.
The proof of Proposition B.6 is reduced to Lemma E.4 and Lemma E.6, which control curvature-amplified terms by moment assumptions and comparison estimates.

source_span: SPD specialization: exponential moment assumptions compensate for curvature growth and yield expected regularity constants.
why_reusable: This gives a blueprint for proving noncompact manifold regularity by pairing curvature comparison with exponential moment assumptions.
input: Hadamard or SPD manifold with curvature bounds
Riemannian Gaussian-like prior
Exponential moment condition on the target distribution
Regularity expressions involving Exp/Log/Jacobian derivatives
output: Expected regularity bounds for vector field and divergence terms
assumptions: Nonpositive curvature and comparison theorem bounds
Exponential moments of target distance are finite
The prior tail is strong enough to control curvature distortion
Derivative bounds for manifold Exp/Log and Jacobian terms are available
core_steps: Express problematic regularity terms through distance and model functions such as s_K(r).
Use comparison theorems to upper-bound curvature-amplified derivatives.
Impose exponential moment conditions that dominate the curvature growth.
Convert pointwise derivative growth into expectation bounds.
Insert these bounds into the abstract Hadamard TV theorem.
Simplify constants to obtain iteration complexity.
limitations: Highly geometry-specific; the SPD proof cannot be copied blindly to arbitrary manifolds.
Requires strong moment assumptions.
Dimension exponents may be loose.

source_blocks: The related-work paragraphs compare Riemannian diffusion, sampling, and Euclidean flow-matching analyses.

why_reusable: Rejected: this is literature positioning, not an operational mathematical proof method.
input: Prior literature
assumptions: Citation context
core_steps: Compare prior methods
limitations: No reusable proof steps
No checkable mathematical transformation
